subtopic: Patterns for Building LLM-based Systems & Products

source.title: Patterns for Building LLM-based Systems & Products
source.author: Eugene Yan (Ziyou Yan)

framing: Seven key patterns organized along two axes: (a) improving performance vs. reducing cost/risk, and (b) closer to the data vs. closer to the user.

patterns.name: Evals
patterns.summary: A set of measurements (benchmark data + metrics) used to assess a model's performance on a task. Foundation for detecting regressions and guiding system change. Yan recommends task-specific evals (Eval-Driven Development) over generic benchmarks like MMLU, and notes the rise of LLM-as-judge (G-Eval, Vicuna paper) with caveats about position, verbosity, and self-enhancement biases.

patterns.name: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)
patterns.summary: Fetches relevant data from outside the foundation model and enhances the input with this data. Grounds the model to reduce hallucination; cheaper to keep a retrieval index fresh than to re-train. Hybrid retrieval (BM25 + dense embeddings) typically beats either alone.

patterns.name: Fine-tuning
patterns.purpose: To get better at specific tasks
patterns.summary: Further training of a pre-trained model on task-specific data. Covers continued pre-training, instruction fine-tuning, single-task fine-tuning, and RLHF. Modern parameter-efficient techniques: soft-prompt tuning, prefix tuning, adapters, LoRA, QLoRA.

patterns.name: Caching
patterns.summary: Store previously computed LLM responses to serve repeat or similar requests quickly. Pure semantic caching is risky (similar prompts may need different answers); safer keys include item IDs, item-ID pairs, and constrained inputs. Can be on-the-fly or pre-computed offline.

patterns.name: Guardrails
patterns.summary: Validate LLM outputs for syntactic correctness, factuality, safety, and adversarial input. Approaches include structural guidance (Microsoft Guidance), Pydantic-style validators (Guardrails), and LLM-based semantic checks (NeMo Guardrails, SelfCheckGPT).

patterns.name: Defensive UX
patterns.summary: Design strategy that assumes ML/LLM output can be wrong. Draws on Human-AI guidelines from Microsoft (18 rules), Google (23 patterns), and Apple. Key moves: set the right expectations, enable efficient dismissal, provide attribution, anchor on familiar UX.

patterns.name: Collect user feedback
patterns.summary: Gather explicit (thumbs up/down, regenerate, edit) and implicit (acceptance, dwell time, downstream actions) feedback to improve evals, fine-tuning data, and guardrails. Examples: ChatGPT thumbs, Midjourney upscale/variation, Copilot accept/edit/ignore.

also_mentioned_briefly_at_end: Data flywheel — continuous data collection improves the model and UX, creating a virtuous cycle
Cascade — break a complex task into simpler subtasks the LLM is good at (e.g., RAG is one instance)
Monitoring — production observability to confirm the AI system is adding value
